insights.title: Unison causes clicks on note-on; fix with short attack
insights.summary: When UnisonoVoiceAmount > 1, each child voice is given a random start offset (up to ~10ms at 44.1kHz) to prevent phase cancellation between unison voices. This offset causes an audible click when no amplitude envelope is present. Adding a ~5ms attack time on the group's gain envelope eliminates the click.

insights.title: Unison voice count changes do not apply to currently held notes
insights.summary: Changing UnisonoVoiceAmount while notes are held has no immediate effect on those voices. Only new note-on events use the updated count. The only workaround is to kill all active notes via scripting and retrigger them.

insights.title: Detune and spread parameters only take effect on new notes
insights.summary: The UnisonoDetune and UnisonoSpread parameter values (and octave/transpose) are read at note-on time and applied to the voice. Changing them during held notes has no effect on those voices. To update these values in real-time, drive them via the Detune Modulation or Spread Modulation chains rather than changing the parameter directly.

insights.title: Child synth pitch modulation chains are bypassed and hidden
insights.summary: When a synth is added as a child of a SynthGroup, its own pitch modulation chain is bypassed and made invisible in the UI. Pitch modulation for all children is driven exclusively by the SynthGroup's shared Pitch Modulation chain. Independent per-child pitch modulation is not possible.

insights.title: Setting child module gain directly causes zipper noise
insights.summary: Changing the Gain parameter of a child module inside a SynthGroup causes audible zipper noise when the change is rapid or modulated. Use a gain modulator (which operates in the 0-1 range, not dB) on the child instead for smooth, artifact-free gain changes.

insights.title: Use MidiMuter to silence children, not module bypass or -100dB gain
insights.summary: To dynamically silence a child synth within a SynthGroup, use a MidiMuter processor rather than bypassing the module or setting its gain to -100dB. A bypassed or silenced module still allocates voices on note-on; a MidiMuter prevents voice allocation entirely, keeping the voice pool available.

insights.title: Monophonic unison: use Legato with Retrigger MIDI script
insights.summary: To achieve monophonic behaviour with unison voices active, add the built-in 'Legato with Retrigger' MIDI script to the SynthGroup. This is the recommended approach; no additional scripting is required.

insights.title: Scriptnode core.pitch_mod does not receive unison detune offsets
insights.summary: Inside a scriptnode network used as a child of a SynthGroup, the core.pitch_mod node reads pitch modulation values before the SynthGroup has applied its per-voice unison detune amounts. The detune offset is therefore not visible to scriptnode nodes. For fully scriptnode-based unison with per-voice detune, use the clone container instead of a SynthGroup.
insights.verify_hint: Check ModulatorSynthGroup.cpp: order of preVoiceRendering vs detune multiplier application to pitch values

insights.title: Clone container as scriptnode alternative to SynthGroup unison
insights.summary: If a sound generator is built entirely in scriptnode, the clone container provides unison-style voice duplication with detune without requiring a SynthGroup wrapper. The author notes it is 'finicky' to configure but avoids the SynthGroup's limitation of no per-voice detune visibility inside scriptnode.

insights.title: Supersaw with samplers: use multiple children with pre-detuned single-cycle waveforms
insights.summary: A practical supersaw/hypersaw effect can be achieved by placing multiple sampler children inside a SynthGroup, each loaded with the same single-cycle waveform at slightly different tuning offsets and non-aligned phase starts. This gives more control over individual voice tuning than the built-in UnisonoDetune linear spread.
